name: Bile acid synthesis (classic and alternative pathways)
summary: Hepatic conversion of cholesterol into the primary bile acids cholic acid and chenodeoxycholic acid via ring hydroxylation/reduction, CYP27A1 side-chain oxidation, peroxisomal beta-oxidation and glycine/taurine conjugation; the major route of cholesterol catabolism, rate-limited by CYP7A1 and controlled by FXR feedback.
description:
Bile acid synthesis is the principal pathway for catabolizing cholesterol (~50% of the ~0.5 g/day cholesterol turned over) and for producing the amphipathic detergents that emulsify dietary lipids and lipid-soluble vitamins.

**Classic (neutral) pathway.** Initiated in the endoplasmic reticulum by **CYP7A1 (cholesterol 7alpha-hydroxylase)** — the rate-limiting, committed step. HSD3B7 then oxidizes/isomerizes the A-ring to the 3-oxo-Delta4 intermediate. **CYP8B1 (sterol 12alpha-hydroxylase)** is the branch point: 12alpha-hydroxylation commits flux to **cholic acid (CA)**, whereas skipping it yields **chenodeoxycholic acid (CDCA)**, so CYP8B1 sets the CA:CDCA ratio. AKR1D1 (Delta4-3-oxosteroid 5beta-reductase) and AKR1C4 (3alpha-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase) fix the 5beta (cis) A/B ring junction and the 3alpha-hydroxyl. The C27 side chain is then oxidized by mitochondrial **CYP27A1** to a C27 acid (THCA/DHCA), activated to a CoA thioester (bile acyl-CoA synthetase, SLC27A5), racemized 25R->25S by AMACR, and shortened by one round of **peroxisomal beta-oxidation** (ACOX2 -> D-bifunctional protein HSD17B4 -> SCPx thiolase) that removes propionyl-CoA to give choloyl-CoA / chenodeoxycholoyl-CoA.

**Alternative (acidic) pathway.** Begins with **CYP27A1** 27-hydroxylation of cholesterol (in many extrahepatic tissues and macrophages), followed by **CYP7B1** oxysterol 7alpha-hydroxylation; it feeds mainly CDCA and is quantitatively minor in healthy adult human liver but important in the neonate and in cholestasis.

**Conjugation.** BAAT amidates the CoA thioesters with glycine or taurine (glyco:tauro ~3:1 in humans), giving conjugated bile salts that are fully ionized at duodenal pH.

**Regulation.** Reabsorbed bile acids activate the nuclear receptor **FXR (NR1H4)**, which (i) induces **SHP (NR0B2)** to inhibit LRH-1/HNF4alpha and repress CYP7A1 and CYP8B1, and (ii) induces intestinal **FGF19 (FGF15)** that signals through hepatic FGFR4/beta-Klotho to repress CYP7A1 — the dominant postprandial feedback in humans. Oxysterols activate **LXRalpha**, which induces CYP7A1 in rodents (but not humans, which lack a functional LXRE in the CYP7A1 promoter). Beyond digestion, bile acids are signaling molecules acting on FXR and the membrane receptor TGR5 to regulate their own synthesis, lipid, glucose and energy metabolism, and they are extensively transformed by the gut microbiome (deconjugation and 7alpha-dehydroxylation to the secondary bile acids deoxycholate and lithocholate).

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acid metabolism and signaling, Compr Physiol 2013 (PMID 23897684)
Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 21 (Lipid biosynthesis / bile acids)

references.title: The enzymes, regulation, and genetics of bile acid synthesis (Russell DW)
Bile acid metabolism and signaling (Chiang JYL)
Bile acids: regulation of synthesis (Chiang JYL)
A regulatory cascade of the nuclear receptors FXR, SHP-1, and LRH-1 represses bile acid biosynthesis (Goodwin B et al.)
Fibroblast growth factor 15 functions as an enterohepatic signal to regulate bile acid homeostasis (Inagaki T et al.)
Cholesterol and bile acid metabolism are impaired in mice lacking the nuclear oxysterol receptor LXR alpha (Peet DJ et al.)
Pleiotropic roles of bile acids in metabolism (de Aguiar Vallim TQ, Tarling EJ, Edwards PA)
Intestinal crosstalk between bile acids and microbiota and its impact on host metabolism (Wahlstrom A et al.)

compartments.name: Endoplasmic reticulum
compartments.description: Microsomal P450s (CYP7A1, CYP8B1, CYP7B1), HSD3B7 and bile acyl-CoA synthetase.

compartments.name: Mitochondrion
compartments.description: Inner-membrane CYP27A1 performs sterol side-chain (C27) oxidation.

compartments.name: Peroxisome
compartments.description: AMACR racemization, ACOX2/HSD17B4/SCPx beta-oxidative side-chain shortening and BAAT conjugation.

compartments.name: Nucleus
compartments.description: FXR/SHP/LRH-1/HNF4alpha/LXRalpha transcriptional control of CYP7A1 and CYP8B1.

compartments.name: Plasma membrane
compartments.description: Hepatic FGFR4/beta-Klotho receptor for the enterokine FGF19.

compartments.name: Extracellular / portal blood
compartments.description: Secreted FGF19 traveling from ileum to liver.

enzymes.name: Bile acyl-CoA synthetase (VLCS/FATP5)
enzymes.family: fatty acid transport protein / acyl-CoA synthetase family

enzymes.name: Fibroblast growth factor 19 (FGF19; FGF15 in rodents)

genes.name: cytochrome P450 family 7 subfamily A member 1
cytochrome P450 family 8 subfamily B member 1
nuclear receptor subfamily 0 group B member 2 (SHP)

reactions.name: Cholesterol 7alpha-hydroxylation (rate-limiting, committed step of the classic pathway)
reactions.equation: Cholesterol + O2 + NADPH + H+ -> 7alpha-Hydroxycholesterol + H2O + NADP+

reactions.name: Sterol 12alpha-hydroxylation (branch-point committing to cholic acid; sets CA:CDCA ratio)
Sterol side-chain oxidation to the C27 acid (three sequential CYP27A1 oxidations: 26-ol -> 26-al -> 26-oate)
reactions.equation: 5beta-Cholestane-3alpha,7alpha,12alpha-triol + 3 O2 + 3 NADPH + 3 H+ -> THCA + 3 NADP+ + 4 H2O

reactions.name: THCA activation to its CoA thioester
Peroxisomal beta-oxidation step 4: thiolytic cleavage releasing propionyl-CoA -> choloyl-CoA
Sterol side-chain oxidation to the C27 acid (CDCA branch; sequential CYP27A1 oxidations)
reactions.equation: 5beta-Cholestane-3alpha,7alpha-diol + 3 O2 + 3 NADPH + 3 H+ -> DHCA + 3 NADP+ + 4 H2O

reactions.name: DHCA activation to its CoA thioester
Peroxisomal beta-oxidation step 1 (CDCA branch): acyl-CoA oxidation
Peroxisomal beta-oxidation step 4 (CDCA branch): thiolytic cleavage -> chenodeoxycholoyl-CoA
Sterol 27-hydroxylation of cholesterol (initiates the acidic/alternative pathway)
reactions.equation: Cholesterol + O2 + NADPH + H+ -> 27-Hydroxycholesterol + H2O + NADP+

reactions.name: 3beta-HSD oxidation/isomerization of 7alpha,27-dihydroxycholesterol (acidic pathway; converges toward CDCA)

regulations.mechanism: Chenodeoxycholic acid is the most potent endogenous agonist of the nuclear bile-acid receptor FXR (NR1H4); reabsorbed bile acids sensed by FXR drive the negative-feedback program.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acid metabolism and signaling, Compr Physiol 2013 (PMID 23897684)

regulations.mechanism: Cholic acid also activates FXR (less potent than CDCA), contributing to bile-acid feedback.
Ligand-activated FXR induces transcription of the atypical orphan receptor SHP (NR0B2) in hepatocytes.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acid metabolism and signaling, Compr Physiol 2013 (PMID 23897684)

regulations.mechanism: SHP heterodimerizes with and inactivates LRH-1 (NR5A2), removing a competence activator of the CYP7A1/CYP8B1 promoters.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acid metabolism and signaling, Compr Physiol 2013 (PMID 23897684)

regulations.mechanism: SHP also antagonizes HNF4alpha transactivation, reinforcing repression of bile-acid synthetic genes.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acids: regulation of synthesis, J Lipid Res 2009 (PMID 19346330)

regulations.mechanism: LRH-1 binds the CYP7A1 promoter and supports its transcription; SHP-mediated inhibition of LRH-1 is a core feedback node.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acid metabolism and signaling, Compr Physiol 2013 (PMID 23897684)

regulations.mechanism: HNF4alpha binds the bile-acid response element of CYP7A1 and activates its transcription.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acids: regulation of synthesis, J Lipid Res 2009 (PMID 19346330)

regulations.mechanism: HNF4alpha is the dominant transcriptional activator of CYP8B1, tuning 12alpha-hydroxylation and thus the cholic-acid:CDCA ratio.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acids: regulation of synthesis, J Lipid Res 2009 (PMID 19346330)

regulations.mechanism: Net effect of the FXR/SHP cascade: repression of CYP7A1, the rate-limiting enzyme (bile-acid negative feedback).

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acid metabolism and signaling, Compr Physiol 2013 (PMID 23897684)

regulations.mechanism: FXR/SHP also represses CYP8B1, lowering the proportion of cholic acid made.

sources.id: Chiang JYL, Bile acids: regulation of synthesis, J Lipid Res 2009 (PMID 19346330)

regulations.mechanism: In the ileum, FXR induces the enterokine FGF19 (FGF15 in rodents), which is secreted into portal blood.
Circulating FGF19 binds hepatic FGFR4 with the co-receptor beta-Klotho, activating ERK/JNK signaling.
FGF19/FGFR4 signaling represses CYP7A1 transcription; this enterohepatic arm dominates postprandial feedback in humans.
27-Hydroxycholesterol and other oxysterols are endogenous agonists of LXRalpha (NR1H3), linking cellular cholesterol/oxysterol load to bile-acid synthesis.
Oxysterol-activated LXRalpha induces CYP7A1 in rodents (feed-forward disposal of cholesterol); the human CYP7A1 promoter lacks a functional LXRE, an important species difference.

relations.note: Cholesterol, the obligate substrate for all bile-acid synthesis, is supplied by de novo cholesterol biosynthesis and lipoprotein-derived cholesterol; bile-acid synthesis is the major route of cholesterol catabolism/elimination.

Peroxisomal shortening of the C27 sterol side chain uses the beta-oxidation machinery (acyl-CoA oxidase, D-bifunctional protein, SCPx thiolase) and releases propionyl-CoA, which is further catabolized.
Taurine used for bile-acid conjugation is derived from cysteine catabolism (cysteine -> cysteinesulfinate -> hypotaurine -> taurine).
Glycine, the predominant conjugation amino acid in humans (glyco:tauro ~3:1), is drawn from serine/glycine one-carbon metabolism.

Gut bacterial bile-salt hydrolases (BSH) deconjugate tauro-/glyco-conjugates to free primary bile acids; anaerobic 7alpha-dehydroxylation (e.g. Clostridium scindens) then yields the secondary bile acid deoxycholate, which is reabsorbed and signals to host FXR/TGR5.
Microbial BSH deconjugation of taurochenodeoxycholate liberates CDCA; 7alpha-dehydroxylation converts it to lithocholic acid, the most hydrophobic/cytotoxic secondary bile acid.
